tournament.name: Founding Fathers Invitational

mvp.name: MVP Award
mvp.description: Awarded to the best player on the winning team

patShanahan.name: Pat Shanahan Award
patShanahan.description: Awarded to the best player on the losing team

tournament.trophy: The Founding Fathers Cup (aka The Goblet of Immortality / The Founders Cup of Pewter and Glory)

events.notes: The one that started it all. In the spring of 2018, Captain Jeff Shanahan and Team Philly hosted Captain Matt Hopkins and Team DC for the inaugural Founding Fathers Invitational — a two-course affair across Applebrook Golf Club and Aronimink Golf Club. There was no trophy yet, no program, no welcome letter. Just two six-man squads, 18 points on the line, and a vague sense that this might become a thing. It became a thing. Team Philly rolled to a 12–6 victory, and Pat Shanahan took home the first-ever MVP award. The rivalry was born. The formalities would follow.
events.weather: Spring conditions in the Philadelphia area.

events.notes: Just six months after losing the inaugural edition, Team DC demanded a rematch — and got one. Dubbed the DC Challenge Match, the 2nd Edition brought the fight to Washington for the first time, a two-course affair across Columbia Country Club and Chevy Chase Club. Team Philly loaded onto a bus in Wayne, PA at 3:30 PM sharp — $110 a head, no jeans allowed — and rolled south to Chevy Chase Club, where rooms were waiting and strategy sessions commenced over dinner. Captain Shanahan assigned rooms with military precision: the Shanahan brothers in one double, the Etherington brothers in the other, Mundy in a single, and Grecco and Nathan sharing a room with 'an extra bed provided.' Luke Etherington drew cooler duty. On the course, Roland 'The Silverback' Miller led Team DC to a 15.5–8.5 victory, earning MVP honors. The series was tied at one apiece, and suddenly this thing was real.
events.weather: Late October in the DC area. Typical fall conditions.

format.handicapRule: 90% for Better Ball/Singles, 30% combined for Alt Shot

events.notes: The 3rd Edition marked the FFI's arrival as a legitimate institution. For the first time, a full tournament program was produced — complete with matchup writeups, course guides, and the kind of commentary that would become an FFI hallmark. The action took place entirely at Applebrook, with 28 points on the line across three rounds: 18 holes of Better Ball in the morning, 9 holes of Singles after lunch, and 9 holes of Alternate Shot to close. The night before, both teams gathered at The Flying Pig in Malvern for drinks and the ceremonial unveiling of pairings. On the course, Ben Etherington was sensational, earning MVP honors as Team Philly cruised to a 17.5–10.5 victory. Behind the scenes, the roster had undergone a quiet revolution — Captain Shanahan had reshuffled Team Philly's lineup, a move that prompted Vice Captain Pat Shanahan to observe: 'Going scorched earth.' Matt Hopkins served as Team DC's Captain. The Founding Fathers Cup, newly made for this edition, had found its first home in Philadelphia.
events.weather: Cool mid-October day in Malvern, PA. High of 59°F, low of 36°F. Very windy conditions throughout the day. Crisp fall golf weather.

events.notes: The year was 2020, and nothing was normal — including the FFI. COVID protocols were in full effect, but the show went on. The 4th Edition brought the tournament back to DC for a two-venue affair: 18 holes of Fourball at Chevy Chase Club in the morning, followed by 9 holes of Fourball and 9 holes of Singles at Columbia Country Club in the afternoon. The night before, Team DC hosted the 1st Annual Crabfeast at the Hopkins Homestead — a tradition that would stick. Two rookies made their FFI debuts: Brian Shevland and Nick Aceto for Team Philly. It would be remembered as the 'Caddy Massacre' year (the details of which remain classified). Matt Hopkins, the hometown Captain, earned MVP honors as Team DC won 14.5–9.5, evening the series at two apiece. Team Philly's bus ride home was reportedly a quiet one.
events.weather: Pleasant early October conditions. Highs around 70°F with comfortable temperatures throughout the day.

format.handicapRule: 90% for Better Ball, 30% combined for Alt Shot

events.notes: The 5th Edition returned to Philadelphia with the series knotted at two apiece. After deliberation with Team DC leadership, all 36 holes were played at Applebrook, and the format featured 18 holes of Better Ball in the morning, 9 holes of Better Ball after lunch, and 9 holes of Alternate Shot to close. The Captain's Dinner was held at Dutton's Barn the night before, where Eagles vs. Buccaneers played on the screens — a fitting backdrop for a room full of men about to go to war. Team DC announced roster shakeups via formal press release: Michael Kearns and Ben Fritz were added, replacing Connolly and Miller, who were injured during a fox hunt at Chevy Chase Club. On the course, it was Devin Tuohey who stole the show, earning MVP as Team DC pulled out a 15–13 nail-biter. DC now led the series 3–2, and the Goblet remained in Washington.
events.weather: Beautiful mid-October day. High of 73°F, low of 45°F. Warm for the season with ideal golf conditions.

events.notes: There are losses, there are blowouts, and then there is the 6th Edition. Team DC delivered the most lopsided result in FFI history — a 21–11 demolition across Columbia Country Club and Chevy Chase Club. Brendan Delany, the perennial Vice Captain with an 18+ handicap and a flair for the dramatic, was named MVP in what remains one of the great underdog performances in tournament lore. Team Philly had made roster moves: Murphy Barton (Bear's brother) and Mark 'Dr. Beeper' Kurd made their FFI debuts, while Rob Nathan was notably absent. None of it helped. The bus ride home to Philadelphia reportedly caused major changes — the kind of organizational soul-searching that precedes either a dynasty or a complete implosion. DC now led the series 4–2, and the Goblet showed no signs of leaving Washington.
events.weather: Cool early October in Chevy Chase, MD. High of 66°F, low of 52°F. Below-average temperatures but dry.

rounds.name: Morning
rounds.pointsBreakdown: 3 pts per match: front + back + total

rounds.name: Afternoon
rounds.pointsBreakdown: 4 pts per match: 1 front, 2 back, 1 total

format.handicapRule: 80% of course handicap, played off the lowest handicap in the group

schedule.event: Aronimink One Day Member Guest Oktoberfest (Shotgun)

events.notes: After three consecutive losses — including the 21–11 humiliation in DC — Team Philly arrived at the 7th Edition with something to prove. The format was stripped down to its purest form: two rounds of 18-hole Better Ball, no singles, no alternate shot. Morning at Applebrook, afternoon at Aronimink. The two-day event opened with lunch and the Aronimink One Day Member-Guest Oktoberfest on Wednesday, followed by the after-party at Everly's Barn. Team DC, staying in the Applebrook Club House, had won three straight and entered with a .571 all-time winning percentage. It didn't matter. Ben 'Two Gloves' Quereau — also known as 2G, Queso, and Q — was magnificent, earning MVP as Team Philly won decisively and snapped the losing streak. The Goblet of Immortality was finally coming home to Philadelphia. The exact score remains a matter of some dispute ('A lot to a little,' per one Team Philly source).
events.weather: Mid-October in the Philadelphia area. Highs around 65°F with typical fall conditions.

format.handicapRule: Fourball Closeout format with max 8 pts per match

events.notes: The 8th Edition broke new ground — literally. For the first time, the FFI left the DC–Philly corridor entirely, venturing south to Farmington Country Club in Charlottesville, Virginia. The two-day format featured Fourball Closeout matches on both Thursday and Friday, with a 3-Club Challenge Exhibition sandwiched in between on Farmington's East Course. There were no caddies (a first), the 10th hole was closed for renovations (forcing creative routing), and the program included a hole-by-hole course guide that read like a travel magazine. Team Philly flew into Charlottesville Airport on Thursday morning, hit the ground running with breakfast and warm-ups, and were on the first tee by 11:40 AM. The evening featured cocktails on the Blue Ridge Terrace followed by a seated dinner in the Pine Room — a far cry from the bus rides and borrowed couches of earlier editions. Mark Kurd returned to the Team Philly roster, and Ben Quereau arrived with a 0.6 handicap, his lowest in FFI history. Results for this edition are still pending official confirmation.
events.weather: Early October in Charlottesville, VA. Highs around 70°F with pleasant autumn weather.

format.handicapRule: 50% combined team course handicap for Foursomes, 90% for Fourball

events.notes: The 9th Edition returned the FFI to Philadelphia for what promised to be the most ambitious edition yet. For the first time, the tournament featured Foursomes (alternate shot off the tee) — a format that demands partnership, trust, and the obligatory "don't be sorry, I am not playing great either" when your partner dumps one in the bunker. The morning round took place at Gulph Mills Golf Club, a new addition to the course rotation, with the afternoon Fourball match at Aronimink Golf Club. The two-day event kicked off Wednesday with the traditional exhibition at Applebrook, including lunch and the Member-Guest. Wayne Lesage made his FFI debut for Team Philly, while Dan Murray joined Team DC — fresh blood on both sides. The apparel gift this year: Peter Millar vests, because nothing says 'we take this seriously' quite like matching outerwear. Team DC went up big in the morning Foursomes only to hang on to win 15-13 after a furious Team Philly comeback. Vince Burke earned MVP honors. Dinner at Aronimink's Belmont Room on Thursday evening crowned Team DC as the champion of the 9th Edition.
events.weather: Mid-October in the Philadelphia area. Highs around 68°F with classic fall golf conditions.

allPlayers.name: Jeff Shanahan
allPlayers.bio: The founding captain of Team Philly and the driving force behind the FFI. Runs the tightest ship in amateur golf — no spot is safe, no debt goes unpaid, and no roster decision is made without his blessing. The self-anointed Head Urban Cowboy leads the Cobbs Creek Foundation off the course and leads with an iron fist wrapped in a Peter Millar quarter-zip on it. Has been known to make shocking roster announcements that leave his own teammates questioning their job security.

allPlayers.name: Pat Shanahan
allPlayers.bio: The other half of the Shanahan dynasty and Team Philly's Vice Captain from day one. The namesake of the Pat Shanahan Award — given to the best player on the losing team — and the 1st Edition MVP. Handles logistics, welcome letters, and expense collection with quiet authority. The always gracious P Shan once tweaked his neck on a morning run before the 2nd Edition and had to emergency-purchase BioFreeze at Wegmans. Together with Jeff, the brothers Shanahan make every roster and strategy decision between them.

allPlayers.name: Will Mundy
allPlayers.bio: Secretary, COO, Director of Transportation, and permanent interim everything. A Senior Vice President at Morgan Stanley's Mundy Wealth Management Group — where alignment is more than something you learn at golf school. Jeff once told Mundy he could see him as Deputy Assistant Captain in two to three years. That was in 2018. Arranged the team's road trips, procured the annual apparel gifts, and had a bunker at Farmington literally renamed 'Case of the Mundies' in his honor. After seven consecutive editions, the long-time Philly veteran was told to retire ahead of the 9th Edition.

allPlayers.name: Ben Etherington
allPlayers.bio: One half of the Everly Brothers and a founding member of Team Philly. Known as Beverly, B Everly, or Phil Everly depending on the program edition. Won MVP at the 3rd Edition after Team Philly's dominant 17.5–10.5 victory at Applebrook. Works at Nathan Trotter & Co. alongside his brother Luke. Famously photographed testing the pewter content of The Goblet of Immortality. His banter-free rounds alongside Pat Shanahan have become the stuff of legend.

allPlayers.name: Luke Etherington
allPlayers.bio: The other Everly Brother and a steadfast member of Team Philly since the 2nd Edition. Also employed at Nathan Trotter & Co., making the Etherington–Nathan Trotter pipeline one of the FFI's most reliable feeder systems. Known for checking the volatile tin market mid-round and for ordering twin lobster tails at the 6th Edition dinner — prompting Jon Carr to respond simply: 'Unbelievable.' Does Hoka make golf shoes? Luke would like to know.

allPlayers.name: Rob Nathan
allPlayers.bio: The Cashmere Queen. A founding member of Team Philly whose wardrobe philosophy is fully aligned with Chevy Chase Club's dress code encouraging cashmere sweaters. Won the Pat Shanahan Award at the 9th Edition (2025) as the best player on the losing side. Once paid his FFI debts at 4:22 AM, suggesting either impressive dedication or alarming insomnia. Jeff's scouting report was characteristically succinct: 'Rob is Rob.' Missed the 6th Edition, prompting the program to mourn the perhaps temporary loss of an FFI stalwart and cashmere aficionado. Need help placing a bet? Call Mr. Cashmere.

allPlayers.name: Ben Quereau
allPlayers.bio: 2G. Queso. Q. A man of many names and even more gloves. Added to the roster ahead of the 3rd Edition, Hopkins himself declared that Team Philly must have Benny Two Gloves on the squad to have a fighter's chance at winning the cup. Captured the 7th Edition MVP as a true competitor and man of integrity. Casual dress at the crabfeast — gloves required, for Quereau.

allPlayers.name: Bear Barton
allPlayers.bio: Berenstain Papa. Featured in what the 2019 program called the primal battle for animal kingdom supremacy — the Bear vs the Silverback — when paired against Roland Miller. A four-time FFI veteran whose steady play and quiet intensity anchored Team Philly's middle order. The 2022 program once asked: is Bear secretly rooting for Team DC, a leadership group clearly more fun to be around? Departed after the 7th Edition, prompting the wistful caption: 'Bear and Murph — we miss you.'

allPlayers.name: Murphy Barton
allPlayers.bio: Brother of Bear and owner of the paralyzing Blue Steel stare. Competed in the 6th Edition — Team DC's historic 21–11 dismantling, the biggest beatdown in tournament history. The Bros Barton were not skerred. Fondly remembered: 'Bear and Murph — we miss you.'

allPlayers.name: Mark Kurd
allPlayers.bio: Doc McStuffins. Dr. Frasier Crane. The FFI's resident physician carries more nicknames than most players carry clubs. The good Dr. Kurd takes time away from the OR to endure four hours of silence next to the always gracious P Shan. Joined the roster in the 6th Edition, missed the 7th, and was warmly welcomed back for the 8th — the 2024 program noting it was the fewest roster changes Team Philly had made in years.

allPlayers.name: Nick Aceto
allPlayers.bio: A roster addition during the great reshuffling era who competed in the 4th and 5th Editions. Paired with Jeff Shanahan against Hopkins and Tuohey at the 5th Edition — directly in the crosshairs of Team DC's leadership.

allPlayers.name: Angelo Grecco
allPlayers.bio: A repeat participant in the early years of the FFI. Once described as a fuzzy Italian feel good guy. Paid his FFI debts 36.2 seconds after the email was sent — a record that will likely never be broken.

allPlayers.name: Kevin Keating
allPlayers.bio: The FFI's one and only in-tournament defector. Originally on Team DC for the inaugural edition — traded live to Team Philly after the match at Aronimink Golf Club in a deal that remains without precedent. A major acquisition for the 2nd Edition, whose entire pairing strategy was built around him — enveloped by Pat in the morning and Grecco in the afternoon. Works at Millcreek Capital. Dropped out of the 3rd Edition due to a nanny crisis and moved out west permanently over concerns about the COVID-19 virus. Was the last Team Philly member to pay his expenses in 2018. Still has not offered Merion access.

allPlayers.name: Brian Shevland
allPlayers.bio: An acting rookie at the 4th Edition in Washington, DC. Paired with Two Gloves Quereau in the first round at Chevy Chase Club. A one-edition appearance in the annals of FFI history.

allPlayers.name: Wayne Lesage
allPlayers.bio: The newest addition to Team Philly for the 9th Edition, replacing the retired Will Mundy. A Philly Mainline classic paired with Mark Kurd for foursomes at Gulph Mills and with Rob Nathan for fourball at Aronimink. Fresh blood for Team Philly's quest to reclaim the Goblet.

allPlayers.name: Matt Hopkins
allPlayers.bio: Captain of Team DC and the FFI's most eloquent communicator — a man whose mock-corporate press releases and formal dispatches have become as essential to the tournament as the golf itself. What Captain Hopkins wants, Captain Hopkins gets. A Managing Director at Wells Fargo, previously at Eastdil Secured. Won the 4th Edition MVP after leading DC to a 14.5–9.5 victory at Chevy Chase and Columbia. Famously photographed in his natural habitat: surrounded by red meat, red wine, hoppy beer, quarter-zips, and deep mahogany.

allPlayers.name: Brendan Delany
allPlayers.bio: Team DC's Vice Captain and one of the tournament's most reliable presences. An attorney at Blank Rome LLP in Washington, DC, whose father — the legendary Dr. Gael Delany — apparently coined the phrase 'fast payments makes fast friends.' Won the 6th Edition MVP after DC's historic 21–11 beatdown, the biggest margin of victory in FFI history. Known for quick-witted email responses, an unwavering commitment to the cause, and strong opinions on beanie-appropriate temperatures for the golf course. Please contact VCBIV for any specific Clubman hygienic product requirements.

allPlayers.name: Jon Carr
allPlayers.bio: Carrski. America's Guest. The self-described resident country club non-member expert and Team DC's Senior Coordinator of Communications, Chairman of Apparel and Logo Creation, and Associate Coordinator of Food and Beverage. Works at Grosvenor. Fresh off an attempt at early retirement, Carr has used his ample time visiting prestigious golf communities. Authored the legendary hole-by-hole course guide in the 2024 welcome program. Once told he was a great 8 but a terrible 6 — and no one has proven the doubters wrong yet. His response to Luke Etherington's twin lobster tail order — 'Unbelievable' — may be the most perfectly concise email in FFI history.

allPlayers.name: Vince Burke
allPlayers.bio: VCB4. Vinny. A Vice President at B.F. Saul Company & Affiliates and a member of the inaugural Team DC roster who hasn't missed an edition since. Longtime bestie of Delaaaahny and a congenial presence known for respectful eye contact and plenty of storytelling. Known for being chronically late on expense payments — a pattern that the tournament committee has noted with characteristic passive aggression. By a wide margin, the friendliest man in the field.

allPlayers.name: Chris Connolly
allPlayers.bio: The best stick in FFI history not named Shanahan is a player who makes the rest of the field look like they're playing a different sport. A senior executive in the insurance industry. Enjoys fine wines and rich steaks. Featured in the classic clash of the titans big dollar match between the brothers Shanahan and Connolly/Keegan at the 3rd Edition. Has never worn pants during an FFI. Wears Brooks Brothers button-down pajamas to bed.

allPlayers.name: Sean Keegan
allPlayers.bio: PM Keegan. AM Keegan. Keegs. The man with the most time-of-day-specific nicknames in amateur golf — his performance and personality shift depending on the decisions made at three imperative milestones: the breakfast table, the shack, and the tap room bar. Runs Rockspring Contracting and once introduced himself to Team Philly: 'I've had braces twice and still have an absurd snaggletooth. My eyebrows are massive and my nose is crooked. Look forward to meeting all you PA boys so you can laugh at my face.' The 2024 program's closing words were a sincere hope that Sean doesn't get them kicked off property.

allPlayers.name: Devin Tuohey
allPlayers.bio: DC's native son who, despite being a member at Philadelphia Country Club, has remained loyal to Team DC through eight editions. Won the 5th Edition MVP after DC's narrow 15–13 victory at Applebrook — winning on enemy soil. Works at the Concordia Group. A perennial point earner known for his black high tops and AND1 shorts, which the 2021 program declared welcome in DC into perpetuity. Missed the 9th Edition for important business reasons — an absence felt by the entire DC roster. If DT needs back, ankle, or emotional support, an alpha male silverback gorilla may not necessarily be the right one to ask.

allPlayers.name: Roland Miller
allPlayers.bio: Silverback is one of the toughest competitors in the rich history of the FFI. He won the 2nd Edition MVP after Team DC's 15.5–12.5 comeback victory at Columbia and Chevy Chase. After missing the 5th Edition in 2021 due to a Fox Hunt injury, he was welcomed back alongside Connolly for the 6th Edition as an old friend. His housekeeper once inspired a Carrski course tip involving the phrase 'no bueno senor Miller.'

allPlayers.name: Michael Kearns
allPlayers.bio: Former childhood actor turned amateur golfer. A long-time member of Columbia Country Club and dedicated pupil of Bob Dolan's 7-4-7 Swing Thought System. Joined Team DC as a replacement for Chris Connolly at the 5th Edition.

allPlayers.name: Ben Fritz
allPlayers.bio: A New England native who joined Team DC as a replacement for Roland Miller at the 5th Edition. Developed his game through decades of intense country club sports at the venerable Bridgehampton Club. Known for volatile play, an inability to show up to tee times on time, and equipment that hasn't been updated since the turn of the millennium — his 35-year-old set of MacGregor's and torn golf gloves somehow work to his advantage. Paired with Devin Tuohey for better ball and alternate shot at Applebrook.

allPlayers.name: Dan Murray
allPlayers.bio: The newest addition to Team DC for the 9th Edition. Paired with Hopkins for fourball at Aronimink and with Connolly for foursomes at Gulph Mills — a debut that places him squarely in the deep end. Fresh blood on the DC side.

allPlayers.name: Robbie Mitchell
allPlayers.bio: A late replacement for Chris Connolly on Team DC at the 4th Edition in 2020. Paired with Vince Burke for best ball at Chevy Chase Club, with Sean Keegan for fourball at Columbia Country Club, and faced Pat Shanahan in singles.
